question: [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged taker, acting as msg.sender in MidnightBundles.buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral, be forced into a DoS when the feeSetter atomically calls setMarketSettlementFee between the TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits view call and the actual Midnight.take execution, causing filledBuyerAssets != targetFilledBuyerAssets and reverting with OutOfOffers, violating the invariant that a valid offer list with sufficient capacity must be fillable to the target? Preconditions: active market, feeSetter is a separate EOA, taker has authorized MidnightBundles, offer list has exactly enough capacity. Call sequence: (1) taker calls buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral, (2) feeSetter front-runs with setMarketSettlementFee raising fee at the relevant breakpoint, (3) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits returns units U based on old fee, (4) Midnight.take executes with new fee yielding buyerAssets != targetFilledBuyerAssets, (5) require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets) reverts. Invariant tested: settlement fee read in TakeAmountsLib must be consistent with fee used in Midnight.take for the same transaction. Scoped impact: fund freeze / low-cost DoS on bundler entry path. Proof idea: fuzz settlementFee delta between view and take, assert OutOfOffers revert frequency.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged maker, whose sell offer has offerPrice near the maximum settlement fee breakpoint, cause sellerAssetsToUnits to return a units value that when passed to Midnight.take yields sellerAssets strictly less than targetSellerAssets after the feeSetter raises the settlement fee to the cap, violating the invariant that sellerAssetsToUnits returns units for which take yields exactly targetSellerAssets? Preconditions: buy offer with offerPrice close to MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_360_DAYS (0.005e18), feeSetter raises fee to cap after view call. Call sequence: (1) sellerAssetsToUnits called with old fee, (2) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee to index 6 at max, (3) Midnight.take uses new sellerPrice = offerPrice - newFee < old sellerPrice, (4) actual sellerAssets < targetSellerAssets. Invariant tested: sellerAssetsToUnits inverse must hold under fee immutability within a single atomic flow. Scoped impact: accounting corruption in MidnightBundles, seller receives fewer assets than expected. Proof idea: unit test with offerPrice = 0.006e18 WAD, fee raised from 0 to 0.005e18, assert sellerAssets discrepancy.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that buyerAssetsToUnits uses mulDivUp(WAD, buyerPrice) for sell offers to return units U such that Midnight.take with U units yields buyerAssets strictly greater than targetBuyerAssets when the feeSetter lowers the settlement fee between the view and the take, causing MidnightBundles to overpay the protocol and under-refund msg.sender? Preconditions: sell offer, feeSetter lowers fee after TakeAmountsLib view, MidnightBundles holds maxBuyerAssets from msg.sender. Call sequence: (1) buyerAssetsToUnits called with high fee, returns U, (2) feeSetter lowers fee, (3) take(U) yields buyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets, (4) filledBuyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets, (5) refund = maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets is reduced. Invariant tested: msg.sender must not pay more than targetBuyerAssets for the filled units. Scoped impact: direct loss to taker via overpayment. Proof idea: fuzz fee decrease magnitude, assert refund shortfall.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause buyerAssetsToUnits to revert with PriceGreaterThanOne when the feeSetter raises the settlement fee for a sell offer such that buyerPrice = offerPrice + newFee > WAD, blocking all MidnightBundles flows that use this offer, violating the invariant that valid offers at valid ticks must remain takeable? Preconditions: sell offer at a tick where tickToPrice returns a price close to WAD (tick near 0), feeSetter raises fee at the 0-day breakpoint to MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS. Call sequence: (1) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS), (2) taker calls buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral, (3) buyerAssetsToUnits computes buyerPrice = offerPrice + fee > WAD, (4) require(buyerPrice <= WAD) reverts. Invariant tested: fee setter must not be able to implicitly cancel valid offers by raising fees above offer price. Scoped impact: fund freeze / offer cancellation without maker consent. Proof idea: fuzz tick in [0..10], fee at max 0-day breakpoint, assert PriceGreaterThanOne revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause sellerAssetsToUnits to panic with division-by-zero when the feeSetter sets the settlement fee equal to offerPrice for a buy offer (sellerPrice = offerPrice - fee = 0), blocking all bundler sell flows for that offer, violating the invariant that valid offers must remain processable by periphery? Preconditions: buy offer with offerPrice = MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_360_DAYS = 0.005e18 (tick near MAX_TICK), feeSetter sets fee at index 6 to 0.005e18. Call sequence: (1) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 6, 0.005e18), (2) sellerAssetsToUnits called for that offer, (3) sellerPrice = 0.005e18 - 0.005e18 = 0, (4) mulDivUp(targetSellerAssets, WAD, 0) panics. Invariant tested: sellerPrice must never be zero for a valid buy offer after fee setter action. Scoped impact: fund freeze on all bundler sell paths for affected offers. Proof idea: unit test with tick = MAX_TICK, fee = tickToPrice(MAX_TICK), assert division-by-zero.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits / sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that TakeAmountsLib reads settlementFee(id, zeroFloorSub(offer.market.maturity, block.timestamp)) at call time, while Midnight.take reads it at execution time, to create a race condition where the feeSetter changes a fee breakpoint between a multicall's first leg (TakeAmountsLib view) and second leg (take), causing the bundler's exact-fill invariant to break? Preconditions: multicall is used to batch TakeAmountsLib view + take, feeSetter is a separate contract that can be triggered mid-multicall via callback. Call sequence: (1) attacker constructs multicall with [touchMarket, buyerAssetsToUnits-equivalent view, take], (2) feeSetter changes fee during a callback in the take leg, (3) units computed in leg 1 no longer yield targetBuyerAssets in leg 3. Invariant tested: fee must be consistent across all legs of a single bundler transaction. Scoped impact: accounting corruption / DoS. Proof idea: stateful fuzz with fee-changing callback, assert filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market with a loanToken for which the feeSetter has set a non-zero defaultSettlementFee, then immediately call buyerAssetsToUnits before any per-market fee override, and observe that the returned units are computed using the default fee, while a subsequent setMarketSettlementFee call by the feeSetter changes the per-market fee, causing a discrepancy between the view result and the take result? Preconditions: defaultSettlementFee[loanToken] is non-zero, market just created, feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee before taker's take. Call sequence: (1) market creator calls touchMarket, (2) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits called with default fee, (3) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee overriding fee, (4) take uses new fee, units from step 2 yield wrong buyerAssets. Invariant tested: default fee and per-market fee must be consistent within a single atomic flow. Scoped impact: DoS / overpayment in bundler. Proof idea: differential test comparing buyerAssets with default vs overridden fee.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the rounding direction in buyerAssetsToUnits (mulDivUp for sell offers) combined with a feeSetter-induced fee increase to cause the computed units to be 1 higher than needed, so that Midnight.take with those units yields buyerAssets = targetBuyerAssets + epsilon, causing filledBuyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets and the bundler's exact-fill check to fail? Preconditions: sell offer, targetBuyerAssets chosen such that mulDivUp rounds up by exactly 1 unit, feeSetter raises fee by 1 CBP. Call sequence: (1) buyerAssetsToUnits returns U = ceil(targetBuyerAssets * WAD / buyerPrice_old), (2) feeSetter raises fee, (3) take(U) with new buyerPrice yields buyerAssets = U * newBuyerPrice / WAD > targetBuyerAssets, (4) filledBuyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets, (5) require fails. Invariant tested: rounding in TakeAmountsLib must not cause overfill under fee changes. Scoped impact: DoS on bundler exact-fill path. Proof idea: fuzz targetBuyerAssets near rounding boundary, assert exact-fill invariant.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the tickSpacingSetter reducing the market's tick spacing between when an offer is signed (at tick T, valid under old spacing S) and when TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits is called, causing the offer to now be at a tick that is a multiple of the new spacing S' < S, but the settlement fee computation in TakeAmountsLib uses the same fee as before, while Midnight.take enforces the new tick spacing check, creating no discrepancy but allowing previously-invalid ticks to be used in new offers that TakeAmountsLib may compute incorrectly? Preconditions: tickSpacingSetter reduces spacing from 4 to 2, new offer at tick 2 is created, TakeAmountsLib called for this offer. Call sequence: (1) tickSpacingSetter calls setMarketTickSpacing(id, 2), (2) maker creates offer at tick 2, (3) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits called, (4) tickToPrice(2) returns a price near WAD, (5) buyerPrice = price + fee may exceed WAD. Invariant tested: tick spacing reduction must not enable offers at prices where buyerPrice > WAD. Scoped impact: PriceGreaterThanOne revert / fund freeze. Proof idea: fuzz tick in [1..3] after spacing reduction, assert buyerPrice <= WAD.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits / sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the coarse-grained authorization model (isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender] grants full control) to call setIsAuthorized on behalf of a victim, then use the victim's authorization to call setMarketSettlementFee via a feeSetter-authorized contract, and then use TakeAmountsLib in a bundler flow where the fee change causes the victim's pending take to revert or overpay? Preconditions: victim has authorized attacker on Midnight, attacker is also feeSetter or can trigger feeSetter. Call sequence: (1) victim authorizes attacker via setIsAuthorized, (2) attacker calls setMarketSettlementFee (if attacker == feeSetter), (3) attacker calls buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral on behalf of victim, (4) TakeAmountsLib uses new fee, (5) victim overpays or transaction reverts. Invariant tested: authorization must not enable fee manipulation that harms the authorizer. Scoped impact: direct loss / DoS for victim. Proof idea: unit test with attacker as both authorized operator and feeSetter.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized allows any currently-authorized address to sign and consume the authorizer's nonce, to front-run a victim's legitimate authorization signature with a malicious one that authorizes a different address, causing the victim's nonce to be incremented and their signature to become invalid, then using TakeAmountsLib in a bundler flow that requires the victim's authorization? Preconditions: victim has signed an EIP-712 authorization for address A with nonce N, attacker is currently authorized by victim, attacker signs a different authorization consuming nonce N. Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization{authorized: A, nonce: N}, (2) attacker (authorized by victim) signs Authorization{authorized: B, nonce: N}, (3) attacker submits their signature first, consuming nonce N, (4) victim's signature is now invalid (InvalidNonce), (5) bundler flow requiring victim's authorization fails. Invariant tested: nonce consumption must be exclusive to the authorizer's own signatures. Scoped impact: DoS on victim's authorization flow / gate bypass. Proof idea: unit test with authorized address front-running nonce consumption.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of a victim use setIsAuthorized to authorize a third address C on behalf of the victim (since authorized addresses can add/remove authorizations), then have C call MidnightBundles functions using TakeAmountsLib on behalf of the victim, effectively creating an unbounded delegation chain that bypasses the victim's intent? Preconditions: victim authorizes attacker, attacker calls setIsAuthorized(C, true, victim), C calls buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with taker=victim. Call sequence: (1) victim calls setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim), (2) attacker calls setIsAuthorized(C, true, victim), (3) C calls buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(taker=victim), (4) TakeAmountsLib computes units, (5) take executes on victim's behalf. Invariant tested: authorization delegation must not be transitive beyond one level without explicit victim consent. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure / direct loss for victim. Proof idea: unit test with 3-level delegation chain, assert victim's position changes without direct consent.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that claimContinuousFee reduces marketState[id].withdrawable and totalUnits, causing a subsequent TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits call to compute units based on a settlement fee that is unchanged, but the actual take may fail due to insufficient withdrawable assets, violating the invariant that a valid take with correct units must succeed? Preconditions: feeClaimer drains continuousFeeCredit to near zero, market has low withdrawable, taker uses bundler. Call sequence: (1) feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee draining withdrawable, (2) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits returns U based on current fee, (3) Midnight.take(U) attempts to transfer sellerAssets but withdrawable is insufficient, (4) take reverts or accounting breaks. Invariant tested: fee claiming must not reduce withdrawable below what is needed for pending takes. Scoped impact: fund freeze / DoS on take path. Proof idea: fuzz continuousFeeCredit claim amount, assert take success after claim.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the piecewise linear interpolation in settlementFee to cause sellerAssetsToUnits to return a units value that is off-by-one at a fee breakpoint boundary (e.g., timeToMaturity = exactly 1 day, 7 days, 30 days), where the fee transitions discontinuously due to integer division, causing the actual sellerAssets from take to differ from targetSellerAssets by 1 unit? Preconditions: offer.market.maturity set such that timeToMaturity is exactly at a breakpoint, feeSetter has set non-uniform fees across breakpoints. Call sequence: (1) block.timestamp = maturity - 1 days exactly, (2) sellerAssetsToUnits called, fee uses breakpoint 0-1 interpolation, (3) block advances 1 second, (4) take called, fee uses breakpoint 1-7 interpolation, (5) sellerAssets differs by rounding. Invariant tested: sellerAssetsToUnits must be consistent with take at the same timestamp. Scoped impact: accounting corruption / DoS at breakpoint boundaries. Proof idea: fuzz timeToMaturity at each of [0, 1d, 7d, 30d, 90d, 180d, 360d] boundaries, assert sellerAssets == targetSellerAssets.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that setDefaultSettlementFee affects only newly created markets (not existing ones) to create a market with a favorable default fee, use TakeAmountsLib to compute units, then have the feeSetter override the per-market fee via setMarketSettlementFee before the take, causing the bundler to compute wrong units and revert? Preconditions: feeSetter sets defaultSettlementFee[loanToken] to 0, attacker creates market (gets 0 fee), feeSetter then calls setMarketSettlementFee to raise per-market fee. Call sequence: (1) feeSetter sets default fee to 0, (2) attacker calls touchMarket (market created with 0 fee), (3) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits called with 0 fee, (4) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee raising fee, (5) take uses new fee, units from step 3 yield wrong buyerAssets. Invariant tested: per-market fee override must not break TakeAmountsLib inverse property. Scoped impact: DoS / overpayment in bundler. [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the roleSetter can replace the feeSetter with address(0) (no validation on newFeeSetter), effectively freezing all future fee changes, and then use TakeAmountsLib in a market where the current fee is at an extreme value that causes buyerPrice > WAD for all offers at low ticks, permanently blocking those offers? Preconditions: feeSetter sets fee to max at 0-day breakpoint, roleSetter sets feeSetter to address(0), offers at low ticks now have buyerPrice > WAD. Call sequence: (1) feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS), (2) roleSetter calls setFeeSetter(address(0)), (3) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits called for offer at tick 0, (4) buyerPrice = tickToPrice(0) + MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS > WAD, (5) require(buyerPrice <= WAD) reverts permanently. Invariant tested: fee setter replacement must not permanently freeze valid offers. Scoped impact: permanent fund freeze on affected offers. Proof idea: unit test with feeSetter=address(0) after max fee set, assert PriceGreaterThanOne for low-tick offers.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits / sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits calls TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits or sellerAssetsToUnits with the current settlement fee, while the feeSetter changes the fee between the consumableUnits call and the actual take in MidnightBundles, causing unitsToTake to be computed incorrectly and the offer to be over- or under-filled relative to its maxAssets cap? Invariant tested: offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity. Scoped impact: offer overfill / accounting corruption. [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that MidnightBundles.buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral uses a try/catch around take, so a fee-induced revert in TakeAmountsLib (PriceGreaterThanOne) is silently swallowed, causing the bundler to skip all offers and then revert with OutOfOffers, while the attacker has already pulled maxBuyerAssets from msg.sender with no refund path? Preconditions: feeSetter raises fee to cause buyerPrice > WAD for all offers in the list, bundler has pulled maxBuyerAssets. Call sequence: (1) pullToken pulls maxBuyerAssets from msg.sender, (2) feeSetter raises fee, (3) for each offer, TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits reverts with PriceGreaterThanOne, (4) try/catch catches revert, filledBuyerAssets stays 0, (5) require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets) reverts, (6) entire tx reverts, funds returned. [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget computes referralFeeAssets = targetSellerAssets * referralFeePct / (WAD - referralFeePct) and then calls sellerAssetsToUnits with targetFilledSellerAssets = targetSellerAssets + referralFeeAssets, so that a feeSetter-induced fee change causes sellerAssetsToUnits to return units that yield filledSellerAssets != targetFilledSellerAssets, causing the require(filledSellerAssets == targetFilledSellerAssets) to revert after collateral has already been supplied? Call sequence: (1) collateral supplied to Midnight, (2) feeSetter changes fee, (3) sellerAssetsToUnits returns U based on old fee, (4) take(U) yields sellerAssets != targetFilledSellerAssets, (5) require fails, tx reverts, (6) collateral is stuck in Midnight for taker. [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits uses zeroFloorSub(offer.market.maturity, block.timestamp) for timeToMaturity, so that at exactly block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity, timeToMaturity = 0 and the fee uses the 0-day breakpoint, while a feeSetter who has set a high 0-day fee causes buyerPrice > WAD for offers at low ticks, permanently blocking post-maturity takes via the bundler? Call sequence: (1) warp to maturity, (2) TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits called, timeToMaturity = 0, fee = settlementFeeCbp0 * CBP = 0.000014e18, (3) buyerPrice = tickToPrice(0) + 0.000014e18, (4) if tickToPrice(0) + 0.000014e18 > WAD, revert. [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits / sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that setMarketContinuousFee changes the continuousFee used in take's buyerPendingFeeIncrease computation, but TakeAmountsLib does not read continuousFee, so a feeSetter-induced continuousFee change between TakeAmountsLib view and take does not affect the units computation but does affect the pendingFee increase, causing the taker's credit position to accrue more fee than expected? [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the roleSetter can atomically replace the feeSetter and then the new feeSetter can change fees, all within a single multicall, to front-run a taker's bundler transaction and cause TakeAmountsLib to compute units based on pre-change fees while take executes with post-change fees? [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that setMarketTickSpacing can only decrease spacing (never increase), so once spacing is reduced to 1, all ticks [0..MAX_TICK] become valid, and TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits can be called for offers at tick 1 or 2 where tickToPrice returns a value very close to WAD, causing buyerPrice = tickToPrice(1) + fee to exceed WAD and revert with PriceGreaterThanOne?
